devanagari: अथ षट्पञ्चाशत्तमोऽध्यायः । श्रीशुक उवाच । सत्राजितः स्वतनयां कृष्णाय कृतकिल्बिषः । स्यमन्तकेन मणिना स्वयमुद्यम्य दत्तवान् ॥ १ ॥
english_devnagari: atha ṣaṭpañcāśattamo'dhyāyaḥśrīśuka uvācasatrājitaḥ svatanayāṃ kṛṣṇāya kṛtakilbiṣaḥsyamantakena maṇinā svayamudyamya dattavān
translation:
Śrī Śuka said:
Satrājita[1] who had committed an offence against Kṛṣṇa, gave, of his own accord and making some special effort for it, his own daughter in marriage to Kṛṣṇa offering therewith the Syamantaka jewel (as an atonement for the offence).
purport:
Footnote [1]:
The word is spelt both ways: Satrājita and Satrājit. The translation retains the spelling adopted in the original Sk. text.

devanagari: श्रीराजोवाच । सत्राजितः किमकरोद्ब्रह्मन्कृष्णस्य किल्बिषः । स्यमन्तकः कुतस्तस्य कस्माद्दत्ता सुता हरेः ॥ २ ॥
english_devnagari: śrīrājovācasatrājitaḥ kimakarodbrahmankṛṣṇasya kilbiṣaḥsyamantakaḥ kutastasya kasmāddattā sutā hareḥ
translation:
King Parīkṣit enquired:
What offence did Satrājita commit against Kṛṣṇa, Oh Brāhmaṇa sage? Whence did he get the Syamantaka gem? Why did he offer his daughter in marriage to Hari?

devanagari: श्रीशुक उवाच । आसीत्सत्राजितः सूर्यो भक्तस्य परमः सखा । प्रीतस्तस्मै मणिं प्रादात्स च तुष्टः स्यमन्तकम् ॥ ३ ॥
english_devnagari: śrīśuka uvācaāsītsatrājitaḥ sūryo bhaktasya paramaḥ sakhāprītastasmai maṇiṃ prādātsa ca tuṣṭaḥ syamantakam
translation:
Śrī Śuka narrated:
The Sun-god was an intimate friend of his own devotee Satrājita. The Sun-god was so much affectionate to and pleased with Satrājita that he presented to him the Syamantaka gem.

devanagari: स तं बिभ्रन्मणिं कण्ठे भ्राजमानो यथा रविः । प्रविष्टो द्वारकां राजन्तेजसा नोपलक्षितः ॥ ४ ॥
english_devnagari: sa taṃ bibhranmaṇiṃ kaṇṭhe bhrājamāno yathā raviḥpraviṣṭo dvārakāṃ rājantejasā nopalakṣitaḥ
translation: Wearing that gem about his neck and effulgent like the Sun-god, he could hardly be visible and recognized due to its dazzling lustre, Oh king.

devanagari: तं विलोक्य जना दूरात्तेजसा मुष्टदृष्टयः । दीव्यतेऽक्षैर्भगवते शशंसुः सूर्यशङ्किताः ॥ ५ ॥
english_devnagari: taṃ vilokya janā dūrāttejasā muṣṭadṛṣṭayaḥdīvyate'kṣairbhagavate śaśaṃsuḥ sūryaśaṅkitāḥ
translation: Beholding him even from a distance, people felt blinded with his brilliant splendour and believing him to be the Sun-god, reported it to the Lord who was playing at dice.

devanagari: नारायण नमस्तेऽस्तु शङ्खचक्रगदाधर । दामोदरारविन्दाक्ष गोविन्द यदुनन्दन ॥ ६ ॥
english_devnagari: nārāyaṇa namaste'stu śaṅkhacakragadādharadāmodarāravindākṣa govinda yadunandana
translation: “Salutations to You, Oh Nārāyaṇa, the Wielder of the conch, discus and mace! Oh Lotus-eyed Dāmodara! Oh Govinda, the Delight of Yadus.

devanagari: एष आयाति सविता त्वां दिदृक्षुर्जगत्पते । मुष्णन्गभस्तिचक्रेण नृणां चक्षूंषि तिग्मगुः ॥ ७ ॥
english_devnagari: eṣa āyāti savitā tvāṃ didṛkṣurjagatpatemuṣṇangabhasticakreṇa nṛṇāṃ cakṣūṃṣi tigmaguḥ
translation: Blinding the eyes of men with his lustre, here comes the Sun-god of scorching rays, with a desire to pay visit to you, Oh Protector of the world.

devanagari: नन्वन्विच्छन्ति ते मार्गं त्रिलोक्यां विबुधर्षभाः । ज्ञात्वाद्य गूढं यदुषु द्रष्टुं त्वां यात्यजः प्रभो ॥ ८ ॥
english_devnagari: nanvanvicchanti te mārgaṃ trilokyāṃ vibudharṣabhāḥjñātvādya gūḍhaṃ yaduṣu draṣṭuṃ tvāṃ yātyajaḥ prabho
translation: Prominent gods really try to seek the way (to find you) in all the three worlds. Having come to know today that you live undisclosed among the Yadus, the birthless Sun-god has come to see you, Oh Lord.”

devanagari: श्रीशुक उवाच । निशम्य बालवचनं प्रहस्याम्बुजलोचनः । प्राह नासौ रविर्देवः सत्राजिन्मणिना ज्वलन् ॥ ९ ॥
english_devnagari: śrīśuka uvācaniśamya bālavacanaṃ prahasyāmbujalocanaḥprāha nāsau ravirdevaḥ satrājinmaṇinā jvalan
translation:
Śrī Suka said:
Hearing the report of the ignorant people, the Lotuseyed Kṛṣṇa laughed loudly and remarked: He is not the Sungod but Satrājit dazzling with the jewel (Syamantaka).

devanagari: सत्राजित्स्वगृहं श्रीमत्कृतकौतुकमङ्गलम् । प्रविश्य देवसदने मणिं विप्रैर्न्यवेशयत् ॥ १० ॥
english_devnagari: satrājitsvagṛhaṃ śrīmatkṛtakautukamaṅgalampraviśya devasadane maṇiṃ viprairnyaveśayat
translation: Satrājit entered his affluent, splendid mansion beautified with auspicious objects (for the celebration of his successful return with the gem) and got the jewel properly installed by Brāhmaṇas in the shrine of his family deities.

devanagari: दिने दिने स्वर्णभारानष्टौ स सृजति प्रभो । दुर्भिक्षमार्यरिष्टानि सर्पाधिव्याधयोऽशुभाः । न सन्ति मायिनस्तत्र यत्रास्तेऽभ्यर्चितो मणिः ॥...
english_devnagari: dine dine svarṇabhārānaṣṭau sa sṛjati prabhodurbhikṣamāryariṣṭāni sarpādhivyādhayo'śubhāḥna santi māyinastatra yatrāste'bhyarcito maṇiḥ
translation: Oh King! Every day, the gem yielded eight bhāras[2] of gold. Where the gem is installed and worshipped, there is no danger from famines, epidemics, calamities, serpents, diseases, inauspicious things or from rogues.
purport:
Footnote [2]:
Bhāvāratha Dīpikā quotes verses which show that one bhāra is equivalent to 8ooo Tolāś (1 Tolā = 11.66 grams).

devanagari: स याचितो मणिं क्वापि यदुराजाय शौरिणा । नैवार्थकामुकः प्रादाद्याच्ञाभङ्गमतर्कयन् ॥ १२ ॥
english_devnagari: sa yācito maṇiṃ kvāpi yadurājāya śauriṇānaivārthakāmukaḥ prādādyācñābhaṅgamatarkayan
translation: He was solicited by Kṛṣṇa on one occasion for the gem, for presenting to Ugrasena, the king of Yādavas. But being greedy of wealth, he did not comply regardless of the consequences of such refusal (to the Lord’s request).

devanagari: तमेकदा मणिं कण्ठे प्रतिमुच्य महाप्रभम् । प्रसेनो हयमारुह्य मृगायां व्यचरद्वने ॥ १३ ॥
english_devnagari: tamekadā maṇiṃ kaṇṭhe pratimucya mahāprabhampraseno hayamāruhya mṛgāyāṃ vyacaradvane
translation: One day, Prasena (the brother of Satrājit) wore that jewel of extra-ordinary lustre about his neck and mounting his horse, he went to the forest for hunting.

devanagari: प्रसेनं सहयं हत्वा मणिमाच्छिद्य केशरी । गिरिं विशन्जाम्बवता निहतो मणिमिच्छता ॥ १४ ॥
english_devnagari: prasenaṃ sahayaṃ hatvā maṇimācchidya keśarīgiriṃ viśanjāmbavatā nihato maṇimicchatā
translation: Having killed Prasena along with his horse, a lion snatched the gem from him (out of curiosity for the dazzling thing) and was entering his mountain cave when he was killed by Jāmbavat who desired to possess the gem.

devanagari: सोऽपि चक्रे कुमारस्य मणिं क्रीडनकं बिले । अपश्यन्भ्रातरं भ्राता सत्राजित्पर्यतप्यत ॥ १५ ॥
english_devnagari: so'pi cakre kumārasya maṇiṃ krīḍanakaṃ bileapaśyanbhrātaraṃ bhrātā satrājitparyatapyata
translation: In his den, he used it as a toy for his young son. Not finding his brother, Satrājit was greatly tormented.

devanagari: प्रायः कृष्णेन निहतो मणिग्रीवो वनं गतः । भ्राता ममेति तच्छ्रुत्वा कर्णे कर्णेऽजपन्जनाः ॥ १६ ॥
english_devnagari: prāyaḥ kṛṣṇena nihato maṇigrīvo vanaṃ gataḥbhrātā mameti tacchrutvā karṇe karṇe'japanjanāḥ
translation: (He expressed his suspicion:) “My brother is most probably murdered by Kṛṣṇa as he went out to the forest with the Syamantaka jewel round his neck”. Hearing that, people passed on the rumour in whispers from ear to ear.

devanagari: भगवांस्तदुपश्रुत्य दुर्यशो लिप्तमात्मनि । मार्ष्टुं प्रसेनपदवीमन्वपद्यत नागरैः ॥ १७ ॥
english_devnagari: bhagavāṃstadupaśrutya duryaśo liptamātmanimārṣṭuṃ prasenapadavīmanvapadyata nāgaraiḥ
translation: Hearing that rumour, the Lord accompanied by some citizens followed the track of Prasena (Prasena’s horse) in order to wipe out the calumny imputed to him.

devanagari: हतं प्रसेनं अश्वं च वीक्ष्य केशरिणा वने । तं चाद्रि पृष्ठे निहतमृक्षेण ददृशुर्जनाः ॥ १८ ॥
english_devnagari: hataṃ prasenaṃ aśvaṃ ca vīkṣya keśariṇā vanetaṃ cādri pṛṣṭhe nihatamṛkṣeṇa dadṛśurjanāḥ
translation: People saw that Prasena and his horse were killed by a lion in the forest and that the lion was killed by a bear on the side of the mountain.

devanagari: ऋक्षराजबिलं भीममन्धेन तमसावृतम् । एको विवेश भगवानवस्थाप्य बहिः प्रजाः ॥ १९ ॥
english_devnagari: ṛkṣarājabilaṃ bhīmamandhena tamasāvṛtameko viveśa bhagavānavasthāpya bahiḥ prajāḥ
translation: Keeping outside the citizens (with instructions to wait), the Lord alone entered the den of the king of bears, though it was terrible and full of blinding darkness.

devanagari: तत्र दृष्ट्वा मणिप्रेष्ठं बालक्रीडनकं कृतम् । हर्तुं कृतमतिस्तस्मिन्नवतस्थेऽर्भकान्तिके ॥ २० ॥
english_devnagari: tatra dṛṣṭvā maṇipreṣṭhaṃ bālakrīḍanakaṃ kṛtamhartuṃ kṛtamatistasminnavatasthe'rbhakāntike
translation: Seeing there that the excellent gem was used as a toy of a male child he determined to take it away and stayed there near the child.[3]
purport:
Footnote [3]:
Subodhinī quotes a verse from Purāṇas as the nurses lullaby to the child, “The lion killed Prasena, the lion was killed by Jāmbavat. Oh. tender child, don’t cry. This Syamantaka is for you.”

devanagari: तमपूर्वं नरं दृष्ट्वा धात्री चुक्रोश भीतवत् । तच्छ्रुत्वाभ्यद्रवत्क्रुद्धो जाम्बवान्बलिनां वरः ॥ २१ ॥
english_devnagari: tamapūrvaṃ naraṃ dṛṣṭvā dhātrī cukrośa bhītavattacchrutvābhyadravatkruddho jāmbavānbalināṃ varaḥ
translation: Seeing that extra-ordinary person (not seen before) the nurse of the child screamed out as in fear. Hearing that, the enraged Jāmbavān who was the foremost among the mighty rushed at him, in rage.

devanagari: स वै भगवता तेन युयुधे स्वामिनाऽत्मनः । पुरुषं प्राकृतं मत्वा कुपितो नानुभाववित् ॥ २२ ॥
english_devnagari: sa vai bhagavatā tena yuyudhe svāminā'tmanaḥpuruṣaṃ prākṛtaṃ matvā kupito nānubhāvavit
translation: Verily, in his indignation, he failed to understand Kṛṣṇa’s greatness, and regarding him as an ordinary person he fought with him, his own Master (Rāma in previous incarnation), the Supreme Lord.

devanagari: द्वन्द्वयुद्धं सुतुमुलमुभयोर्विजिगीषतोः । आयुधाश्मद्रुमैर्दोर्भिः क्रव्यार्थे श्येनयोरिव ॥ २३ ॥
english_devnagari: dvandvayuddhaṃ sutumulamubhayorvijigīṣatoḥāyudhāśmadrumairdorbhiḥ kravyārthe śyenayoriva
translation: Like two hawks fighting for a piece of meat, the combat between them was tumultuous as they tried to overthrow each other by weapons, rocks, trees and arms.

devanagari: आसीत्तदष्टाविंशाहमितरेतरमुष्टिभिः । वज्रनिष्पेषपरुषैरविश्रममहर्निशम् ॥ २४ ॥
english_devnagari: āsīttadaṣṭāviṃśāhamitaretaramuṣṭibhiḥvajraniṣpeṣaparuṣairaviśramamaharniśam
translation: There was a continuous combat for twenty-eight days (and nights as well) hitting each other with fists, harsh like the strokes of thunderbolt.

devanagari: कृष्णमुष्टिविनिष्पात निष्पिष्टाङ्गोरु बन्धनः । क्षीणसत्त्वः स्विन्नगात्रस्तमाहातीव विस्मितः ॥ २५ ॥
english_devnagari: kṛṣṇamuṣṭiviniṣpāta niṣpiṣṭāṅgoru bandhanaḥkṣīṇasattvaḥ svinnagātrastamāhātīva vismitaḥ
translation: Jāmbavān was extremely wonderstruck as his joints and limbs were crushed and dislocated by the blows of Kṛṣṇa’s fists and as his strength decreased and the body was full of perspiration. He addressed to him:

devanagari: जाने त्वां सर्वभूतानां प्राण ओजः सहो बलम् । विष्णुं पुराणपुरुषं प्रभविष्णुमधीश्वरम् ॥ २६ ॥
english_devnagari: jāne tvāṃ sarvabhūtānāṃ prāṇa ojaḥ saho balamviṣṇuṃ purāṇapuruṣaṃ prabhaviṣṇumadhīśvaram
translation: “(Now) I know You to be Lord Viṣṇu, the Ancient- most Person, the Creator of the Universe and the Controller thereof Who constitute the energy, the power of endurance (mental strength) and physical power of all beings.

devanagari: त्वं हि विश्वसृजां स्रष्टा सृष्टानामपि यच्च सत् । कालः कलयतामीशः पर आत्मा तथात्मनाम् ॥ २७ ॥
english_devnagari: tvaṃ hi viśvasṛjāṃ sraṣṭā sṛṣṭānāmapi yacca satkālaḥ kalayatāmīśaḥ para ātmā tathātmanām
translation: You are really the Creator of the progenitors of the Universe; You are the Reality that underlies it (as the material cause of the created substances). You are the Time Spirit which controls and destroys the world. You are the Supreme Soul of all.

devanagari: यस्येषदुत्कलितरोषकटाक्षमोक्षैर् । वर्त्मादिशत्क्षुभितनक्रतिमिङ्गलोऽब्धिः । सेतुः कृतः स्वयश उज्ज्वलिता च लङ्का । रक्षःशिरांसि भुवि पेत...
english_devnagari: yasyeṣadutkalitaroṣakaṭākṣamokṣairvartmādiśatkṣubhitanakratimiṅgalo'bdhiḥsetuḥ kṛtaḥ svayaśa ujjvalitā ca laṅkārakṣaḥśirāṃsi bhuvi peturiṣukṣatāni
translation: You are verily my Lord Rāma, at whose side-glances which were slightly enkindled with wrath, the whole ocean full of alligators, whales and other aquatic animals got agitated and was made to give way to you; Who built a bridge across the sea as if it was a memorial of your glory; who burnt down the city of Laṅkā and by whose arrows, the heads of demons rolled on the ground.”

devanagari: इति विज्ञातविज्ञानमृक्षराजानमच्युतः । व्याजहार महाराज भगवान्देवकीसुतः ॥ २९ ॥
english_devnagari: iti vijñātavijñānamṛkṣarājānamacyutaḥvyājahāra mahārāja bhagavāndevakīsutaḥ
translation: To his devotee, the king of bears who had thus realized the knowledge of his being the Supreme Spirit, Kṛṣṇa, that Imperishable glorious Lord, the lotus-eyed son of Devakī, touched (all over his body, out of compassion) with his bliss-bestowing hand (relieving all his fatigue, wounds and pain in the combat) and out of Supreme Mercy and Grace, spoke to him in a voice deep with affection.

devanagari: अभिमृश्यारविन्दाक्षः पाणिना शंकरेण तम् । कृपया परया भक्तं मेघगम्भीरया गिरा ॥ ३० ॥
english_devnagari: abhimṛśyāravindākṣaḥ pāṇinā śaṃkareṇa tamkṛpayā parayā bhaktaṃ meghagambhīrayā girā
translation: To his devotee, the king of bears who had thus realized the knowledge of his being the Supreme Spirit, Kṛṣṇa, that Imperishable glorious Lord, the lotus-eyed son of Devakī, touched (all over his body, out of compassion) with his bliss-bestowing hand (relieving all his fatigue, wounds and pain in the combat) and out of Supreme Mercy and Grace, spoke to him in a voice deep with affection.

devanagari: मणिहेतोरिह प्राप्ता वयमृक्षपते बिलम् । मिथ्याभिशापं प्रमृजन्नात्मनो मणिनामुना ॥ ३१ ॥
english_devnagari: maṇihetoriha prāptā vayamṛkṣapate bilammithyābhiśāpaṃ pramṛjannātmano maṇināmunā
translation: “We (I and citizens of Dvārakā) arrived here at the entrance of your cave for the sake of the Syamantaka gem. (Though some of the false imputation of calumny is hereby removed), I have entered the den to wipe out completely the false accusation (of Prasena’s murder) against me, by means of (i.e. by producing) this gem.”

devanagari: इत्युक्तः स्वां दुहितरं कन्यां जाम्बवतीं मुदा । अर्हणार्थं स मणिना कृष्णायोपजहार ह ॥ ३२ ॥
english_devnagari: ityuktaḥ svāṃ duhitaraṃ kanyāṃ jāmbavatīṃ mudāarhaṇārthaṃ sa maṇinā kṛṣṇāyopajahāra ha
translation:
It is traditionally reported that thus addressed, Jāmbavān gladly offered his daughter Jāmbavatī in marriage to Kṛṣṇa, along with the Syamantaka gem, by way of worshipping him.
[Bhāgavata Candrikā’s Text adds:]
32(A). Lord Kṛṣṇa who is affectionate to his devotees, was worshipped (by Jāmbavān) with many other excellent precious stones. And the Lord instantly bestowed on him (or decided to confer upon him) the Supreme Emancipation from which there is no return to saṃsāra.

devanagari: अदृष्ट्वा निर्गमं शौरेः प्रविष्टस्य बिलं जनाः । प्रतीक्ष्य द्वादशाहानि दुःखिताः स्वपुरं ययुः ॥ ३३ ॥
english_devnagari: adṛṣṭvā nirgamaṃ śaureḥ praviṣṭasya bilaṃ janāḥpratīkṣya dvādaśāhāni duḥkhitāḥ svapuraṃ yayuḥ
translation: After Waiting (at the entrance of the cave) for twelve days and finding that Kṛṣṇa entered the cave but had not returned out of if, the people who accompanied him there, returned to their own city (Dvārakā) in grief.

devanagari: निशम्य देवकी देवी रुक्मिण्यानकदुन्दुभिः । सुहृदो ज्ञातयोऽशोचन्बिलात्कृष्णमनिर्गतम् ॥ ३४ ॥
english_devnagari: niśamya devakī devī rukmiṇyānakadundubhiḥsuhṛdo jñātayo'śocanbilātkṛṣṇamanirgatam
translation: When they heard the report that Kṛṣṇa had not issued out of the cave (for the last twelve days), Devakī (Kṛṣṇa’s mother), queen Rukmiṇī, Vasudeva (Kṛṣṇa’s father), his friends and kinsmen deeply grieved (his non-return).

devanagari: सत्राजितं शपन्तस्ते दुःखिता द्वारकौकसः । उपतस्थुश्चन्द्र भागां दुर्गां कृष्णोपलब्धये ॥ ३५ ॥
english_devnagari: satrājitaṃ śapantaste duḥkhitā dvārakaukasaḥupatasthuścandra bhāgāṃ durgāṃ kṛṣṇopalabdhaye
translation: Citizens of Dvārakā cursed Satrājit (for the disappearance of Kṛṣṇa). Deeply grieving, they devotedly worshipped the goddess Durgā (well-known by the name, Candrabhāgā),[4] the Supreme Illusive potency of the Lord, for the safe return of Śrī Kṛṣṇa.
purport:
Footnote [4]:
Bhāvāratha Dīpikā remarks: [candrabhāgāṃ nāma durgām] suggests that his Text most probably read Candrabhāgā for Mahāmāyā and was [upatasthus candrabhāgāṃ durgām] etc. instead of the current one.

devanagari: तेषां तु देव्युपस्थानात्प्रत्यादिष्टाशिषा स च । प्रादुर्बभूव सिद्धार्थः सदारो हर्षयन्हरिः ॥ ३६ ॥
english_devnagari: teṣāṃ tu devyupasthānātpratyādiṣṭāśiṣā sa caprādurbabhūva siddhārthaḥ sadāro harṣayanhariḥ
translation: (Possibly) due to the blessing of the goddess they received through their worship and prayers of the deity, Lord Hari manifested Himself along with his spouse (Jāmbavatī) and with his purpose (recovery of the Syamantaka gem) completely achieved, thus thrilling them with joy.

devanagari: उपलभ्य हृषीकेशं मृतं पुनरिवागतम् । सह पत्न्या मणिग्रीवं सर्वे जातमहोत्सवाः ॥ ३७ ॥
english_devnagari: upalabhya hṛṣīkeśaṃ mṛtaṃ punarivāgatamsaha patnyā maṇigrīvaṃ sarve jātamahotsavāḥ
translation: All of them were transported with ecstatic joy to find Kṛṣṇa as if returned to life after death and that too with a spouse and the Syamantaka gem about his neck.

devanagari: सत्राजितं समाहूय सभायां राजसन्निधौ । प्राप्तिं चाख्याय भगवान्मणिं तस्मै न्यवेदयत् ॥ ३८ ॥
english_devnagari: satrājitaṃ samāhūya sabhāyāṃ rājasannidhauprāptiṃ cākhyāya bhagavānmaṇiṃ tasmai nyavedayat
translation: Summoning Satrājit to the royal assembly, in the presence of the king (Ugrasena), the Lord narrated the story of the recovery of the gem and entrusted the gem to his (Satrā- jit’s) care.

devanagari: स चातिव्रीडितो रत्नं गृहीत्वावाङ्मुखस्ततः । अनुतप्यमानो भवनमगमत्स्वेन पाप्मना ॥ ३९ ॥
english_devnagari: sa cātivrīḍito ratnaṃ gṛhītvāvāṅmukhastataḥanutapyamāno bhavanamagamatsvena pāpmanā
translation: Deeply feeling ashamed, he took that Symantaka jewel. Casting down his head and full of repentance for his guilt (of traducing the Lord), he returned home.

devanagari: सोऽनुध्यायंस्तदेवाघं बलवद्विग्रहाकुलः । कथं मृजाम्यात्मरजः प्रसीदेद्वाच्युतः कथम् ॥ ४० ॥
english_devnagari: so'nudhyāyaṃstadevāghaṃ balavadvigrahākulaḥkathaṃ mṛjāmyātmarajaḥ prasīdedvācyutaḥ katham
translation: Constantly brooding over the sinful act (of caluminating an innocent person like Kṛṣṇa) and being agitated and. perplexed at his conflict with a powerful party, he pondered: “How can I wipe off this misdeed and misbehaviour? Or how will Lord Kṛṣṇa be propitiated and gracious unto me?

devanagari: किं कृत्वा साधु मह्यं स्यान्न शपेद्वा जनो यथा । अदीर्घदर्शनं क्षुद्रं मूढं द्रविणलोलुपम् ॥ ४१ ॥
english_devnagari: kiṃ kṛtvā sādhu mahyaṃ syānna śapedvā jano yathāadīrghadarśanaṃ kṣudraṃ mūḍhaṃ draviṇalolupam
translation: What course would lead to my well-being whereby people won’t censure me who am really short-sighted, mean, foolish and greedy of wealth.

devanagari: दास्ये दुहितरं तस्मै स्त्रीरत्नं रत्नमेव च । उपायोऽयं समीचीनस्तस्य शान्तिर्न चान्यथा ॥ ४२ ॥
english_devnagari: dāsye duhitaraṃ tasmai strīratnaṃ ratnameva caupāyo'yaṃ samīcīnastasya śāntirna cānyathā
translation: I shall offer him my daughter who is a jewel among women, as well as that Syamantaka gem to him. This is the proper expedient, otherwise no peace can be brought about (between us) by any other way.”

devanagari: एवं व्यवसितो बुद्ध्या सत्राजित्स्वसुतां शुभाम् । मणिं च स्वयमुद्यम्य कृष्णायोपजहार ह ॥ ४३ ॥
english_devnagari: evaṃ vyavasito buddhyā satrājitsvasutāṃ śubhāmmaṇiṃ ca svayamudyamya kṛṣṇāyopajahāra ha
translation: As the tradition reports, by this reasoning process,he reached this decision. Taking personal initiative in the matter, Satrājit offered his daughter who was excellent in all respects as well as the Syamantaka jewel to him.

devanagari: तां सत्यभामां भगवानुपयेमे यथाविधि । बहुभिर्याचितां शील रूपौदार्यगुणान्विताम् ॥ ४४ ॥
english_devnagari: tāṃ satyabhāmāṃ bhagavānupayeme yathāvidhibahubhiryācitāṃ śīla rūpaudāryaguṇānvitām
translation: According to ritualistic formalities prescribed in the Śāstras the Lord married Satyabhāmā who was blessed with good disposition, charming beauty, generosity and other excellent virtues and had many suitors soliciting her hand.

devanagari: भगवानाह न मणिं प्रतीच्छामो वयं नृप । तवास्तां देवभक्तस्य वयं च फलभागिनः ॥ ४५ ॥
english_devnagari: bhagavānāha na maṇiṃ pratīcchāmo vayaṃ nṛpatavāstāṃ devabhaktasya vayaṃ ca phalabhāginaḥ
translation:
(Declining the offer of the Syamantaka gem) the glorious Lord said, “We do not accept the Syamantaka gem. Let it be with you as you are the devotee of the Sun-god (the donor of the gift). We shall enjoy the yield of the gem (viz. gold. As you have no son. I am the legal heir for the gold, yielded everyday by the gem).
[back to top]
